student_name: Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari
hub_subtitle: Inglês do dia a dia — apresentar-se, rotina e conversação

lesson.menu_title: Saying More With Less -- Ellipsis and Substitution in Everyday Conversation
lesson.menu_desc: Sound natural by saying less: cohesion words that keep a real American conversation flowing -- so, one, do so, neither and either -- plus everyday chat vocabulary like small talk, casual, awkward, to nod and to sum up -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- Saying More With Less
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Paula Porto de Oliveira Fornazari | Lesson 16 -- Saying More With Less
lesson.grammar_point: ellipsis and substitution (so, one, do so, neither/either)

vocab.title: Match each conversation word to its meaning
defs: light, friendly conversation about the small, everyday things in life
to talk with someone in a friendly, relaxed way
to stop someone while they are speaking
to move your head up and down to show you agree
to say something briefly, without many details
short, using only a little time or a few words
to say the main points in a short, clear way
in the same way; the same is true for me
to continue talking or doing something

paras: Last weekend I met my neighbor Rita at the bakery, and we stopped for a brief chat. At first it was just small talk -- the weather, the weekend, nothing serious. I am usually a little nervous about casual conversations in English, because I am afraid of an awkward silence. But this time the talk just flowed, and I did not want it to stop.

The strange thing is that a good conversation often uses fewer words, not more. When Rita said, 'I love the coffee here,' I did not repeat the whole sentence; I simply said, 'So do I.' When she asked if the bakery was open on Sundays, I answered, 'I think so,' instead of 'I think the bakery is open on Sundays.' She wanted a small cake, and the man behind the counter only asked, 'Which one?' Nobody said the long version, and nobody needed to. We left the obvious words out, and the conversation felt lighter and faster.

Rita never interrupted me when I searched for a word, and I did the same for her. At one point she mentioned that she does not enjoy big, noisy parties, and I nodded and said, 'Neither do I.' We agreed on almost everything. To sum up, I learned something simple that day: to sound natural, you do not have to say more -- often you say less. You can leave out the obvious part, use a short word like 'so' or 'one,' and just go on talking. Likewise, when someone gives you that space, the chat feels easy, and nobody feels awkward.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 reading, a learner discovers how ellipsis and substitution make everyday conversation flow

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: It is a step-by-step guide on how to order cakes and coffee at a bakery.
Ana Paula discovers that natural conversation often uses fewer words, by leaving out the obvious part and using short words like 'so' and 'one'.
It argues that small talk in English is impossible and should always be avoided.

items: At first, Ana Paula and Rita talked about serious, important topics.
The text says: 'At first it was just small talk -- the weather, the weekend, nothing serious.'
Instead of repeating the whole sentence, Ana Paula answered, 'So do I.'
The text says: 'I simply said, So do I.'
Rita interrupted Ana Paula every time she searched for a word.
The text says: 'Rita never interrupted me when I searched for a word.'
Ana Paula and Rita both dislike big, noisy parties.
The text says Rita 'does not enjoy big, noisy parties, and I nodded and said, Neither do I.'

parts: Rita said she loved the bakery, and I said, 'So
I.' She asked if it was open on Sunday, and I answered, 'I think
.' She wanted a cake, so she pointed at a small
. I do not like big parties, and
does she. She gave me space to find my words, and,
, I did the same for her.

gapfill.text: To sound natural, English often leaves out words you can guess and replaces them with a short one. 'So' stands for a whole idea (I think so = I think it is open). 'One' stands for a noun (a small one = a small cake). 'Do / does / did' stands for a verb phrase (So do I = So do I love it). 'Neither' and 'either' answer a negative idea (Neither do I = I do not either). This is called ellipsis and substitution: you say less, but you mean just as much.

bank.label: Short answers that keep a conversation flowing
items: I love this place. -- So do I.
I do not like crowds. -- Neither do I.
Is it open on Sunday? -- I think so.
I hope the bus is on time. -- I hope so too.
Which cake do you want? -- The small one, please.
I have not seen that show. -- Neither have I.
She was tired, and so was I.
Thanks for waiting. -- Likewise, thanks for your patience.

lf.title: What does the short word replace?
items: Is the store open? -- I think
I need a pen. -- I have

lf.text: Notice the pattern: 'so' replaces a whole idea, 'one' replaces a noun, 'do / did' replaces a verb phrase, and 'neither / either' answers a negative. After 'so' and 'neither' at the start, the verb comes before the subject: So do I, Neither can I. This inversion is what makes the short answer sound natural.

items.situation: A friend says, 'I really love this coffee.' You agree, using a short answer with 'so'.
tips: So do I -- it is really good.

items.situation: A friend says, 'I do not enjoy loud parties.' You agree, using a short answer with 'neither'.
tips: Neither do I -- I prefer quiet places.

items.situation: Someone asks, 'Is the pharmacy open on Sundays?' You are not completely sure. Answer with 'so'.
tips: I think so, but I am not sure.

items.situation: At a bakery, the server asks, 'Which cake would you like?' Point to a small cake, using 'one'.
tips: I would like the small one.

items.situation: A friend says, 'I have not tried that restaurant yet.' You have not either. Answer with 'neither'.
tips: Neither have I -- let us go together.

items: I will say four short sentences. Answer each one with a natural short answer: 'I love this city.' / 'I do not like the cold.' / 'Is the cafe open?' / 'Which coffee do you want?'
Have thirty seconds of small talk with me about your weekend. When we agree, use 'So do I' or 'Me too'; when we agree on a negative, use 'Neither do I.'
Describe a recent friendly chat you had. Use at least three short forms (so, one, do so, neither) instead of repeating whole sentences.
A new colleague makes small talk with you in the hallway. Keep the conversation going for a minute, sound relaxed, and leave out the obvious words.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model answers
list: So: replaces a whole idea. 'Is it open? -- I think so.' 'I love it. -- So do I.'
One / ones: replaces a noun. 'Which cake? -- The small one.' 'I like the blue ones.'
Do / does / did: replaces a verb phrase. 'She sings, and so do I.' 'He left early, and I did too.'
Neither / either: answers a negative. 'I cannot go. -- Neither can I.' 'I do not like it, and she does not either.'
The trick: leave out the words you can guess, and use one short word instead. You say less, but you mean just as much.
answerkey.note: Ellipsis and substitution make your English sound natural and fluent. Learn the four short words -- so, one, do, neither/either -- and you will keep any conversation flowing.

listenings.text: People think good conversation is about having a lot to say, but honestly, it is often the opposite. The best talkers I know say less, not more. When I agree with someone, I do not repeat their whole sentence. If a friend says he loves a restaurant, I just say, So do I. If someone asks whether the shop is open, I say, I think so, and that is enough. When I do not like something and my friend does not either, I say, Neither do I, and we both smile. The little words carry the meaning: so, one, do, neither. You leave out the obvious part, and the conversation moves faster and feels warmer. The funny thing is, saying less actually makes people feel more connected to you, not less.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I just wanted to tell you about the nicest little chat I had this morning. I ran into my new neighbor at the mailbox, and we made small talk for about five minutes. It was so easy and casual. When I said I loved the quiet street, she said, So do I, and when I said I did not enjoy the traffic downtown, she said, Neither do I. We agreed on everything without saying much at all! She asked if the corner cafe was any good, and I just said, I think so. To sum up, it was a two-minute conversation, but I walked away feeling like I had made a friend. Anyway, call me back when you can. Talk soon, bye!

extra_audio.text: I met my neighbor Rita for a brief chat at the bakery. At first we just made small talk about the weekend. When she said she loved the coffee, I said, So do I. She asked if the shop was open on Sunday, and I said, I think so. To sum up, the whole conversation felt easy and natural.
